message:
fix(fcp-intercom): split contacts.delete behind dedicated capability (resolves br-5g8rj)

Resolves flywheel_connectors-5g8rj — [REVIEW-A9][P2] connector
authorization: `intercom.contacts.delete` was gated by the generic
`intercom.contacts.write` capability shared with contact creation.

**Previous behavior (over-broad capability):**

Both metadata builders — the `Introspection` shape returned by
`handle_introspect` (line ~441) AND the JSON metadata returned by
`operations_info()` (line ~872) — declared
`contacts.delete.capability = "intercom.contacts.write"`. That is
the same string used by `contacts.create`. A token issued for a
workflow that legitimately needs to create contacts could ALSO
delete them — even though `contacts.delete` is `RiskLevel::High` /
`SafetyTier::Dangerous` / irreversible, while `contacts.create` is
`RiskLevel::Medium` / `SafetyTier::Risky` / reversible.

Same defect class as br-m7s4q (datadog monitor.delete) and br-crktb
(hubspot). Violates least authority for destructive operations.

**Fix:**

Three coordinated edits — the bead explicitly called out
"introspection AND operations_info AND handshake":

1. `handle_handshake` capability list (line 246+): adds
   `intercom.contacts.delete` to the advertised capabilities so
   callers can request the destructive scope explicitly during
   preflight / token issuance.
2. `Introspection::operations` `OperationInfo` (line ~448): the
   delete operation now requires capability id
   `"intercom.contacts.delete"` (was `"intercom.contacts.write"`).
3. `operations_info()` JSON metadata (line ~875): mirror of the
   introspection-side change. Both metadata builders MUST agree;
   the test below catches drift.

Comments at all three sites name the bead and explain the
least-authority split.

**Regression test (1 new):**

`operations_contacts_delete_has_dedicated_capability` locks in
that `contacts.delete.capability == "intercom.contacts.delete"`,
`contacts.create.capability == "intercom.contacts.write"`, and
the two are NOT the same string. A future regression that
re-conflates them fails at test time.

236 fcp-intercom tests passing (207 lib + 29 integration; +1 new
lib test vs. pre-fix).

**Operator action:**

Existing tokens that named only `intercom.contacts.write` will no
longer authorize contact deletion — desired behavior; that was
the bug. Workflows that actually need to delete contacts must
update token issuance to request `intercom.contacts.delete`
explicitly.

Pattern: same as br-m7s4q (datadog monitor.delete, 7593eedd) and
br-crktb (hubspot). Defense-in-depth for destructive actions.

files.patch:
@@ -244,6 +244,11 @@ impl IntercomConnector {
             "capabilities": [
                 "intercom.contacts.read",
                 "intercom.contacts.write",
+                // br-5g8rj: contact deletion is irreversible
+                // (RiskLevel::High / SafetyTier::Dangerous) and is
+                // gated by a dedicated capability. A token issued
+                // for the create/update workflow cannot also delete.
+                "intercom.contacts.delete",
                 "intercom.conversations.read",
                 "intercom.conversations.write",
                 "intercom.tags.read"
@@ -438,7 +443,16 @@ impl IntercomConnector {
                         "properties": {"contact_id": {"type": "string"}}
                     }),
                     output_schema: json!({"type": "object"}),
-                    capability: CapabilityId::from_static("intercom.contacts.write"),
+                    // br-5g8rj: split destructive contacts.delete behind
+                    // its own capability id. Pre-fix used the generic
+                    // intercom.contacts.write (same id as
+                    // contacts.create), so a token issued to a
+                    // create/update workflow could ALSO delete contacts —
+                    // RiskLevel::High + SafetyTier::Dangerous +
+                    // irreversible. Holders that legitimately need
+                    // delete semantics must request the dedicated
+                    // capability explicitly.
+                    capability: CapabilityId::from_static("intercom.contacts.delete"),
                     risk_level: RiskLevel::High,
                     description: None,
                     rate_limit: None,
@@ -869,7 +883,9 @@ fn operations_info() -> serde_json::Value {
         {
             "id": "intercom.contacts.delete",
             "summary": "Delete a contact",
-            "capability": "intercom.contacts.write",
+            // br-5g8rj: dedicated capability for irreversible delete —
+            // mirrors the introspect-side OperationInfo at line ~441.
+            "capability": "intercom.contacts.delete",
             "risk_level": "high",
             "safety_tier": "dangerous",
             "idempotency": "strict",
@@ -1397,6 +1413,49 @@ mod tests {
         assert_eq!(op["risk_level"], "high");
     }
 
+    /// br-5g8rj: contacts.delete must require a DEDICATED capability,
+    /// not the generic contacts.write shared with create/update.
+    /// Locks in the split across BOTH the introspection-side
+    /// OperationInfo and the operations_info() JSON metadata
+    /// builder so a future regression that re-conflates them is
+    /// caught at test time.
+    #[test]
+    fn operations_contacts_delete_has_dedicated_capability() {
+        let ops = operations_info();
+        let arr = ops.as_array().unwrap();
+
+        let delete = arr
+            .iter()
+            .find(|op| op["id"] == "intercom.contacts.delete")
+            .expect("contacts.delete operation must be present");
+        assert_eq!(
+            delete["capability"].as_str().unwrap(),
+            "intercom.contacts.delete",
+            "delete must require its own capability id, not the generic write",
+        );
+        // Sanity: still high-risk + dangerous.
+        assert_eq!(delete["risk_level"], "high");
+        assert_eq!(delete["safety_tier"], "dangerous");
+
+        // create still routes through generic write — only delete
+        // is split out.
+        let create = arr
+            .iter()
+            .find(|op| op["id"] == "intercom.contacts.create")
+            .expect("contacts.create operation must be present");
+        assert_eq!(
+            create["capability"].as_str().unwrap(),
+            "intercom.contacts.write",
+            "create still uses the generic write capability",
+        );
+        assert_ne!(
+            create["capability"].as_str().unwrap(),
+            delete["capability"].as_str().unwrap(),
+            "create and delete must require different capabilities",
+        );
+
+    }
+
     #[test]
     fn operations_conversations_reply_not_idempotent() {
         let ops = operations_info();
